pattern: Treating each (node, last-edge-color) as a distinct BFS state lets a single breadth-first search find the shortest color-alternating distance to every node.
hints: The validity of your next move depends on the color of the edge you just used, so a node's state is not just the node but the node paired with that last color.
Run BFS from node 0 over states (node, color); from a state you may only follow edges of the opposite color.
Start with both (0, red) and (0, blue) at distance 0; for each node the answer is the smaller of the two state distances, or -1 if neither was reached.

edge_inputs.description: two red edges cannot chain so node 2 is unreachable
blue edge does not originate from the path so node 2 unreachable
single node, only the trivial path
alternating red then blue reaches node 2
self loop and parallel edges do not break BFS
both colors from source give distance one to the same node

stress.description: random directed red/blue graphs over up to 100 nodes
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    rcount = rng.randint(0, min(400, n * 3))
    bcount = rng.randint(0, min(400, n * 3))
    red = [[rng.randrange(n), rng.randrange(n)] for _ in range(rcount)]
    blue = [[rng.randrange(n), rng.randrange(n)] for _ in range(bcount)]
    return [n, red, blue]
stress.note: self-edges and parallel edges occur naturally
